refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two concerns: (1) the functional requirement to enable teleoperation communication, and (2) a performance constraint on latency. The refined FR isolates the core functional obligation (enable teleoperation) while deferring the quantitative latency threshold to acceptance criteria and design constraints. This keeps the FR solution-neutral and problem-focused: the system must support remote operation capability; how fast that communication must be is a design constraint driven by safety analysis, not part of the functional definition itself. The phrase 'compatible with safe remote operation' remains qualitative at the FR level, allowing the DP and acceptance criteria to bind it to the p95 ≤ 150 ms metric.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the teleoperation communication path is established end-to-end (operator input → system → equipment feedback) and that latency measurements under agreed network conditions meet the p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion. Confirm through safety case review that this latency threshold supports safe operation per worksite operational policies.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit operator commands from the tele-operator to the remote equipment.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment state and sensor feedback from the remote equipment to the tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall ensure teleoperation communication latency is compatible with safe remote operation as defined in the worksite safety plan.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency budgeting across acquisition, transmission, processing, and actuation stages, using priority-queued message handling and bounded processing windows to meet p95 ≤ 150 ms.

DP2: Deploy a dual-channel redundant communication architecture (primary + failover) with independent latency-optimized paths and automatic switchover logic to ensure safe operation under network degradation.

DP3: Establish a hierarchical control model with local autonomous safety enforcement at the remote site (independent of uplink latency) and supervisory teleoperation commands from the operator, decoupling safety from communication latency.

DP4: Use adaptive bitrate encoding and selective data transmission (prioritize critical control signals over non-essential telemetry) to minimize payload size and transmission latency under variable network conditions.

DP5: Implement a time-synchronized, predictive state estimation layer at the remote site that tolerates transient communication delays by extrapolating operator intent and maintaining safe operational bounds without waiting for every command acknowledgment.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3 (Hierarchical Control Model with Local Safety Enforcement) combined with DP1 (Deterministic Low-Latency Pipeline) as the primary solution architecture. DP3 decouples safety-critical constraints from communication latency by enforcing hard safety rules locally; DP1 ensures the teleoperation command path itself meets the 150 ms p95 requirement. DP2 (redundancy) and DP4 (adaptive encoding) are complementary tactical DPs to be layered as secondary resilience measures.

potential_sources: DP1 and DP3 are loosely coupled: DP1 optimizes the communication pipeline latency; DP3 ensures that even if DP1 occasionally exceeds 150 ms, the system remains safe. This is a healthy separation of concerns.
DP4 (adaptive bitrate) may introduce complexity in DP1's latency budgeting if not carefully isolated; recommend DP4 operate as a transparent layer below the deterministic pipeline.
DP2 (redundancy) adds operational complexity but does not create functional coupling if failover logic is cleanly encapsulated.
coupling_check.notes: The selected combination avoids the anti-pattern of relying solely on communication latency to enforce safety. By delegating safety to local autonomous enforcement (DP3), the design is robust to transient latency spikes. DP1 then focuses purely on optimizing the nominal teleoperation path, reducing coupling between safety and performance.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Local autonomous safety enforcement at the remote site is technically and operationally feasible (e.g., geofencing, force limits, emergency stop logic can be implemented and validated independently of the teleoperation link).
ASSUMPTION: The worksite safety plan and operational policies define clear boundaries for what constitutes 'safe' autonomous behavior in the absence of operator commands.

RISK: If local safety enforcement is incomplete or incorrectly specified, the system may permit unsafe actions even with perfect teleoperation latency. Mitigation: Rigorous safety case and independent verification of local safety logic.

RISK: Adaptive bitrate encoding (DP4) may introduce non-deterministic latency if not carefully bounded. Mitigation: Define hard upper bounds on encoding/decoding time and test under worst-case network conditions.

RISK: Dual-channel redundancy (DP2) increases system complexity and cost; may not be justified if single-channel DP1 + DP3 already meets safety requirements. Mitigation: Conduct failure mode analysis to determine if redundancy is necessary or if DP3's local safety is sufficient.

RISK: The p95 ≤ 150 ms criterion assumes symmetric latency and does not account for jitter or packet loss. Mitigation: Clarify acceptance criteria to include jitter bounds and packet loss tolerance; design DP1 to handle transient packet loss gracefully.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine the FR to explicitly state: 'The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms AND shall enforce safety-critical constraints (e.g., motion limits, geofencing, emergency stop) locally at the remote site, independent of uplink latency.' This revision makes explicit the decoupling of safety from communication performance and clarifies that DP3 (local safety) is a mandatory architectural principle, not optional. Additionally, specify acceptance criteria for jitter (e.g., p99 latency, maximum latency spike) and packet loss tolerance to enable rigorous DP1 design and testing.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates the hierarchical control model (DP3) with local safety enforcement and the deterministic low-latency command pipeline (DP1). Owns the real-time command path from operator input through safety-gated actuation, including local constraint validation, latency-bounded scheduling, and safety state machine logic.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and high-level task directives from the human operator or task scheduler; entry point for DP1 pipeline initiation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits safety constraint violations and local enforcement decisions; allows Safety Services to monitor and react to locally-enforced safety rules.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with Vehicle; carries deterministic command packets (out) and telemetry feedback (in) within the 150 ms p95 latency budget.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives vehicle state and sensor data to inform local safety checks and control loop feedback within DP3's hierarchical model.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. DP3 (hierarchical control with local safety) and DP1 (deterministic low-latency pipeline) are both fundamentally teleoperation concerns. The module owns the command path, safety state machine, and real-time scheduling. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert interface requires coordination with Safety Services (external), and Network Link depends on vehicle-side implementation, but these are expected cross-module dependencies.

Poor fit for primary DP ownership. Safety Services should monitor and validate safety rules, not own the real-time control pipeline. DP1 (low-latency deterministic pipeline) would diffuse heavily into Teleoperation and Vehicle modules. DP3's local enforcement logic would be split between Safety Services (policy) and Teleoperation (execution), violating cohesion.

Poor fit. Vehicle should own actuation and low-level motor control, not the teleoperation command hierarchy or safety enforcement logic. DP1 and DP3 are operator-side concerns; placing them in Vehicle creates tight coupling and violates separation of concerns. Significant diffusion of control logic across network boundary.

Poor fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not a specific functional module. Assigning DP3 and DP1 to NGHE Solution would leave ownership ambiguous and prevent clear interface contracts. DP2 (redundancy) and DP4 (adaptive encoding) might be coordinated at this level, but the primary control logic must be owned by a more specific module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP3 (Hierarchical Control Model with Local Safety Enforcement) and DP1 (Deterministic Low-Latency Pipeline) because: (1) it is the logical locus of operator-to-vehicle command translation; (2) local safety enforcement (DP3) must execute in the teleoperation path to meet hard real-time constraints without network round-trips; (3) the deterministic pipeline (DP1) is inherently a teleoperation concern, managing command scheduling and latency budgets; (4) the module's external interfaces (Tasking in, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout, Telemetry in) align naturally with the DP's information flows. DP2 (redundancy) and DP4 (adaptive encoding) are secondary resilience tactics that layer onto this primary architecture without requiring ownership changes.
containment_risks: Network Link interface introduces a hard dependency on Vehicle-side implementation; if Vehicle does not support the expected command/feedback protocol, DP1's latency guarantees may be violated. Mitigation: establish strict interface contract and latency SLA on Network Link.

SafetyAlert interface creates a dependency on Safety Services' ability to react to local enforcement decisions in real time. If Safety Services is slow to process alerts, the system may not achieve coordinated safety posture. Mitigation: define alert priority levels and asynchronous processing guarantees.

Telemetry input dependency: if vehicle telemetry is delayed or lossy, DP3's local safety checks may operate on stale state. Mitigation: design DP3 to be robust to bounded telemetry latency and define fallback safe states.

DP2 (redundancy) and DP4 (adaptive encoding) may require coordination across Teleoperation and Network Link layers; ensure these secondary DPs do not introduce latency or coupling that undermines DP1's determinism. Mitigation: layer these DPs as orthogonal concerns with clear activation conditions.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The selected combination of DP3 + DP1 (primary) with DP2 + DP4 (secondary) is sound and maps cleanly to the Teleoperation module boundary. However, to strengthen containment and reduce diffusion risk, consider: (1) explicitly defining the Teleoperation module's internal architecture as a two-layer stack: a real-time deterministic command scheduler (DP1 realization) sitting above a hierarchical safety state machine (DP3 realization); (2) formalizing the Network Link interface contract to include latency bounds, packet ordering guarantees, and graceful degradation modes; (3) establishing a clear protocol for SafetyAlert emission and acknowledgment to ensure Safety Services can monitor without blocking the real-time path. These refinements improve DP-to-IM traceability without changing the fundamental module boundary.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR requirement (end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency ≤ 150 ms p95 for safe remote operation) is directly measurable and traceable to the Teleoperation IM boundary. The module owns the deterministic command pipeline (DP1) and hierarchical safety enforcement (DP3), both of which are necessary and sufficient to satisfy the FR. Verification can be conducted via: (1) latency profiling of the command path from operator input through safety-gated actuation; (2) stress testing under network conditions specified in constraints; (3) safety state machine validation against worksite safety plan policies. The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is quantifiable and testable within the Teleoperation module's scope.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The FR is unambiguously allocated to the Teleoperation module. The module's four external interfaces (Tasking in, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout, Telemetry in) form a complete and coherent information contract that supports both the functional requirement and the underlying DPs. No ambiguity exists regarding which module is responsible for meeting the latency and safety constraints. The allocation is consistent with the containment hypothesis and the module's natural role as the operator-to-vehicle command translator.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP combination (DP3 + DP1 primary, DP2 + DP4 secondary) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. DP1 (Deterministic Low-Latency Pipeline) ensures the command path meets the 150 ms p95 latency requirement through real-time scheduling and bounded-latency communication. DP3 (Hierarchical Control Model with Local Safety Enforcement) decouples safety-critical constraints from network latency by enforcing hard safety rules locally, eliminating the need for round-trip safety validation and enabling safe remote operation even under transient network degradation. DP2 (redundancy) and DP4 (adaptive encoding) provide secondary resilience without compromising the primary latency or safety guarantees. The logic is sound: deterministic latency + local safety enforcement = safe teleoperation. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Network Link interface creates a hard dependency on Vehicle-side protocol compliance and latency behavior. If the Vehicle module does not implement the expected command/feedback packet structure or introduces unexpected delays, DP1's deterministic latency guarantees may be violated. Risk level: Medium. Mitigation: establish a formal interface contract (SLA) on Network Link specifying packet format, ordering guarantees, maximum round-trip latency, and graceful degradation modes. Validate Vehicle implementation against this contract before integration.

SafetyAlert interface introduces an asynchronous dependency on Safety Services' ability to process and react to locally-enforced safety decisions. If Safety Services is slow to acknowledge alerts or cannot keep pace with high-frequency safety events, the system may fail to achieve coordinated safety posture across modules. Risk level: Medium. Mitigation: define alert priority levels (critical, warning, info), establish non-blocking asynchronous processing guarantees, and design DP3 to operate independently of Safety Services acknowledgment (i.e., local enforcement must not wait for external validation).

Telemetry input dependency: DP3's hierarchical safety model relies on vehicle state and sensor data to inform local safety checks. If telemetry is delayed, lossy, or stale, safety decisions may be made on outdated information, potentially violating worksite safety plan constraints. Risk level: Medium-High. Mitigation: design DP3 to be robust to bounded telemetry latency (e.g., assume worst-case delay equal to network round-trip time); define fallback safe states that activate if telemetry is not received within a specified window; validate telemetry freshness before using it in safety checks.

DP2 (redundancy) and DP4 (adaptive encoding) may introduce secondary latency or coupling if not carefully layered. For example, redundant command transmission or adaptive encoding negotiation could add jitter or scheduling overhead that undermines DP1's determinism. Risk level: Low-Medium. Mitigation: design DP2 and DP4 as orthogonal concerns with clear activation conditions (e.g., DP2 activates only on detected packet loss; DP4 activates only when network bandwidth is constrained); ensure these secondary DPs do not introduce blocking or synchronization points in the primary command path.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment. The Teleoperation module achieves a 0.92 containment score with only 0.08 diffusion. DP3 and DP1 are both fundamentally teleoperation concerns and are naturally owned by this module. The module's internal architecture should be explicitly structured as a two-layer stack: (1) Real-Time Deterministic Command Scheduler (DP1 realization): manages command queuing, latency-bounded scheduling, and packet transmission with hard timing guarantees; (2) Hierarchical Safety State Machine (DP3 realization): enforces local safety constraints, validates commands against worksite safety plan policies, and emits SafetyAlert signals. This internal decomposition improves traceability and allows independent testing of each DP. The four external interfaces are well-defined and create clear boundaries with neighboring modules (Tasking, Safety Services, Vehicle, Telemetry sources). No evidence of DP logic leaking into other modules or of ownership ambiguity. The module boundary is cohesive and maintainable.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the Teleoperation module's internal architecture as an explicit two-layer stack: (1) Deterministic Command Scheduler (DP1 realization) and (2) Hierarchical Safety State Machine (DP3 realization). Document the internal interface between these layers to clarify information flow and timing constraints.

Establish a formal interface contract (SLA) for the Network Link interface specifying: packet format, ordering guarantees, maximum round-trip latency, packet loss tolerance, and graceful degradation modes. This contract must be validated against Vehicle-side implementation before integration testing.

Define a SafetyAlert protocol that specifies: alert priority levels (critical, warning, info), asynchronous processing guarantees, and non-blocking emission semantics. Ensure that local safety enforcement (DP3) does not wait for Safety Services acknowledgment; alerts are emitted asynchronously and Safety Services reacts opportunistically.

Design DP3's safety state machine to be robust to bounded telemetry latency. Specify: (1) maximum acceptable telemetry age before fallback safe state activation; (2) fallback safe state behavior (e.g., command inhibition, safe shutdown); (3) telemetry freshness validation logic. Validate this design against worst-case network latency scenarios.

Document the activation conditions and interaction semantics for DP2 (redundancy) and DP4 (adaptive encoding) to ensure they do not introduce latency jitter or coupling that undermines DP1's determinism. Consider these as orthogonal resilience tactics that layer onto the primary architecture without modifying the command path.

Conduct a latency budget analysis that allocates the 150 ms p95 requirement across the Teleoperation module's internal components (command parsing, safety validation, scheduling, transmission) and the Network Link (round-trip latency). Validate that the sum of allocated budgets does not exceed the overall requirement under worst-case conditions.

Establish a test plan that verifies: (1) DP1 latency compliance under nominal and degraded network conditions; (2) DP3 safety enforcement correctness against worksite safety plan policies; (3) DP2 and DP4 activation and interaction without latency regression; (4) end-to-end FR satisfaction (safe teleoperation with p95 ≤ 150 ms latency).

decision.justification: The FR-DP-IM gates all pass with high confidence, and the Teleoperation module achieves excellent containment (0.92) with clear ownership and interface boundaries. However, the safety criticality of this system (high) and the medium-to-medium-high diffusion risks identified in the DP-IM gate—particularly around telemetry freshness, network latency dependency, and safety state machine robustness—warrant explicit decomposition of the two primary DPs (DP1: Deterministic Low-Latency Pipeline and DP3: Hierarchical Safety Enforcement) into sub-level FRs. This decomposition will: (1) make the internal architecture of the Teleoperation module explicit and traceable; (2) allocate latency and safety budgets to specific sub-components; (3) enable independent verification of each DP's realization; (4) reduce integration risk by clarifying the contract between the command scheduler and safety state machine. The recommended revisions (formal two-layer stack, interface contracts, latency budget analysis, safety state machine robustness) are best realized through sub-FR decomposition rather than revision at the current level. Stopping here would leave critical architectural decisions (e.g., telemetry freshness validation, latency allocation, safety state machine design) unresolved and increase integration risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Ensure deterministic command scheduling with bounded latency ≤ 75 ms p95 (allocated from 150 ms p95 overall requirement, accounting for safety validation and network round-trip time).
FR1.2: Enforce hierarchical safety constraints locally without external validation, ensuring all commands comply with worksite safety plan policies before transmission.
FR1.3: Validate telemetry freshness and activate fallback safe states if telemetry is stale or unavailable, ensuring safety decisions are made on current vehicle state.
FR1.4: Manage redundant command transmission (DP2) and adaptive encoding (DP4) as orthogonal resilience tactics without introducing latency jitter or coupling into the primary command path.
FR1.5: Establish and maintain formal interface contracts (SLAs) with Network Link, SafetyAlert, and Telemetry inputs, specifying packet formats, latency bounds, and graceful degradation modes.

revise.why: No revision of the current FR, DP, or IM is required. The gates pass and the logic is sound. Decomposition is the appropriate next step to manage complexity and reduce integration risk.

assumptions: The Vehicle module will implement the Network Link interface according to a formal SLA contract specifying packet format, ordering guarantees, and maximum round-trip latency.
Safety Services can process SafetyAlert signals asynchronously without blocking the Teleoperation module's command path.

Telemetry sources (vehicle state, sensor data) will provide updates at a frequency sufficient to support real-time safety validation; worst-case telemetry latency is bounded by the network round-trip time.
The worksite safety plan policies can be encoded as a hierarchical state machine with deterministic evaluation time (sub-millisecond per command).
The operator's input device and the Teleoperation module's command parser introduce negligible latency (< 5 ms) relative to the 150 ms p95 budget.

Network latency is the dominant contributor to end-to-end latency; the Teleoperation module's internal processing (scheduling + safety validation) can be bounded to ≤ 50 ms p95 to leave margin for network round-trip time.
open_risks: Telemetry staleness: If telemetry latency exceeds the assumed bound or if telemetry updates are lossy, the safety state machine may make decisions on outdated vehicle state, violating safety constraints. Mitigation: FR1.3 must define explicit telemetry freshness validation and fallback safe state activation.

Network Link interface contract violation: If the Vehicle module does not implement the expected packet structure, ordering guarantees, or latency bounds, DP1's deterministic latency guarantees may be violated. Mitigation: FR1.5 must establish a formal SLA and validation protocol.

Safety state machine complexity: If the worksite safety plan policies are complex or poorly specified, the hierarchical state machine may be difficult to implement deterministically or may introduce unexpected latency. Mitigation: FR1.2 must include a design constraint that the safety state machine evaluation time is bounded and predictable.

Redundancy and adaptive encoding coupling: If DP2 and DP4 are not carefully layered, they may introduce latency jitter or synchronization overhead that undermines DP1's determinism. Mitigation: FR1.4 must specify orthogonal activation conditions and non-blocking semantics.

Integration testing complexity: Verifying that the Teleoperation module meets the 150 ms p95 latency requirement under realistic network conditions (packet loss, jitter, congestion) requires sophisticated test infrastructure and may reveal latency budget overruns late in development. Mitigation: Conduct latency budget analysis (recommended revision) and establish a test plan early.
evidence_needed: Latency budget analysis allocating the 150 ms p95 requirement across Teleoperation module components (command parsing, safety validation, scheduling, transmission) and Network Link round-trip time. Validate that allocated budgets are achievable with selected technologies (e.g., real-time OS, deterministic network protocol).

Formal specification of the Network Link interface contract (SLA) including packet format, ordering guarantees, maximum round-trip latency, packet loss tolerance, and graceful degradation modes. Validate Vehicle-side implementation against this contract.
Specification of the worksite safety plan policies and their encoding as a hierarchical state machine. Analyze state machine complexity and verify that evaluation time is bounded and deterministic.
Design of the telemetry freshness validation logic and fallback safe state activation. Specify maximum acceptable telemetry age and fallback behavior (e.g., command inhibition, safe shutdown).

Test plan for DP1 (deterministic scheduling) including latency profiling under nominal and degraded network conditions, stress testing with high command frequency, and validation of latency budget allocation.

Test plan for DP3 (hierarchical safety enforcement) including validation of safety state machine correctness against worksite safety plan policies, testing of telemetry freshness validation and fallback activation, and end-to-end safety scenario testing.

Test plan for DP2 and DP4 (redundancy and adaptive encoding) including verification that activation conditions are met, that secondary DPs do not introduce latency regression, and that interaction semantics are correct.
Integration test plan validating end-to-end FR satisfaction (safe teleoperation with p95 ≤ 150 ms latency) under realistic network conditions and worksite safety constraints.
